behaviors.description: The review input is scoped to lines that changed, not full file content
behaviors.justification: Requires verifying diff parsing against real GitHub diff format. A unit test with manually crafted diffs could miss GitHub-specific nuances (context lines, file headers, binary detection). The failure mode—processing unchanged lines or skipping changed ones—could hide in mock formats but surface with real PR diffs that have edge cases like renames or mode changes.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR with multi-line changes, fetch the actual GitHub unified diff, pass it to the review agent, and verify the agent only analyzes lines marked with +/- prefix (changed lines), excluding context lines and headers. Verify against the parsed diff structure that no unchanged lines are processed.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A unit test with good fixtures could partially verify diff parsing, but Integration is needed because real GitHub diff edge cases (renames, deletions, line continuations) could break parsing in ways mocks don't expose.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are attached to correct positions in the diff view
behaviors.justification: GitHub's diff position numbering is relative to the patch, not absolute line numbers. Off-by-one errors and confusion between absolute/relative positions would fail silently with mocked APIs (which would accept any position). The failure mode is 'comment posts to wrong line' which requires real GitHub to verify the position is both valid AND semantically correct (attaches to intended code).
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR with specific code changes, calculate the diff position for a target line using the agent's logic, post an inline comment via the agent, fetch the PR's comments via GitHub API, and verify the comment appears attached to the intended changed line (not a nearby context line), validating the position against GitHub's actual position numbering.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test could verify position calculation logic in isolation, but would not catch bugs where the calculated position number is off-by-one in a way GitHub rejects, or where context vs. changed line distinction is wrong. Real GitHub API is necessary.

behaviors.description: Agent identifies at least one real defect in a PR containing a known introduced bug
behaviors.justification: Mocking the LLM hides the real failure mode: the actual model failing to recognize a real bug. A unit test with mock LLM would always 'find' the bug in a hardcoded response, passing even if the real LLM cannot identify it. The failure mode is 'LLM does not recognize the bug', which only surfaces when the real model analyzes the code.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR with an intentional bug (e.g., unchecked null dereference, missing error handling), call the agent with a real LLM API key to analyze the diff, capture the findings, and verify that the specific defect type appears in the agent's identified issues. Run this against multiple known bugs to ensure the LLM's analysis generalizes.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Neither unit nor integration with mocked LLM can detect this failure—the test would pass with a mock that always returns findings. Agentic level (real LLM) is non-negotiable; the behavior explicitly requires a real model to identify real defects.

behaviors.description: Every finding in summary comment has a corresponding inline comment posted
behaviors.justification: While the consistency logic could be tested at unit level with mock findings, the failure mode is 'code posts N inline comments but summary lists M findings' where N ≠ M. This requires either (1) careful unit testing with varied input sizes, or (2) integration testing where we post and verify against real GitHub. Integration is more robust because it tests the full flow with realistic LLM output that might trigger off-by-one errors in iteration or indexing that unit tests miss.
behaviors.test_description: Run the agent on a test PR to generate findings and post both inline comments and a summary comment. For each finding in the summary comment, verify an inline comment was posted at the corresponding location. For each posted inline comment, verify it is mentioned in the summary. Capture both via GitHub API and cross-check them.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A unit test with 5 mock findings could catch iteration bugs (e.g., off-by-one in a loop that skips the last finding). Integration is chosen because real LLM output with variable counts is more likely to expose skipped findings or mismatch bugs than a fixed set of mock findings.

behaviors.description: Full sequence completes without manual intervention for a real pull request
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires the entire agent pipeline to execute end-to-end: read GitHub diff → call real LLM for analysis → post multiple comments to GitHub → post summary. Failures can occur at any step (LLM timeout, GitHub rate limit, malformed LLM output, network interruption, ordering dependency). Unit/Integration with mocked LLM would miss LLM failures; System with mocked LLM would miss LLM failures; Agentic without real GitHub would miss API failures. Only Workflow (full pipeline with real LLM and real GitHub) catches cross-step failures and coordination issues.
behaviors.test_description: Select or create a real test PR, invoke the agent to read the diff, analyze it with a real LLM, post inline review comments, and post a top-level summary comment. Verify the agent completes without crashes, exceptions, or timeouts. Fetch the PR from GitHub and verify all comments are present at the expected positions, demonstrating the full sequence succeeded end-to-end.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System level (real GitHub) would miss real-LLM failures; Agentic (real LLM) would miss GitHub API failures if mocked. The 'full sequence' requirement and 'without manual intervention' demand that both real LLM and real GitHub work in concert. Workflow is the correct minimum.
